Autodesk Build vs Procore (2026)

The two dominant platforms for commercial construction are competing for the same customers. Here's the honest breakdown.

✓ Updated April 2026

Short answer: If your company is deep in the Autodesk design ecosystem (Revit, Civil 3D, Navisworks), Autodesk Build is the more natural choice and will have better BIM coordination. If you're in a mixed or Autodesk-light environment, Procore's larger ecosystem and more mature financial tools give it the edge for most commercial GCs.

Side-by-side comparison

Feature Autodesk Build Procore
Pricing Custom — contact sales Custom — contact sales
Target market Commercial + infrastructure GCs Commercial GCs of all sizes
Drawing management Excellent — best-in-class Excellent — very strong
BIM integration Native (Revit, Navisworks, C3D) Via integration partners
RFI management Strong Industry-leading
Submittal tracking Strong Industry-leading
Financial management Good (add-on module) More mature — industry standard
Field issues Excellent — plan-linked Excellent
Integration ecosystem Growing (~200 integrations) Largest (~500+ integrations)
US market share Significant — growing Largest in commercial
Mobile app Strong Strong
Learning curve Moderate-high Moderate-high

Where Autodesk Build has the edge

BIM and design integration

If your project team uses Revit for structural or architectural design, Civil 3D for site work, or Navisworks for clash detection, Autodesk Build connects to those tools natively. Field issues can be linked to model elements, design revisions flow directly into the drawing set, and the transition from design coordination to construction execution happens within the same Autodesk ecosystem. For BIM-heavy commercial projects, this integration removes a real friction point.

Document management

Autodesk Build's document management (powered by Autodesk Docs) is arguably the best in class for version control, access control, and transmittal workflows. Every document version is tracked, older versions are archived but searchable, and access permissions can be granular by user and document type. For projects with multiple design teams and complex document hierarchies, this depth matters.

Infrastructure and heavy civil

Autodesk has stronger tooling for civil and infrastructure work (roads, utilities, bridges) through its Civil 3D and InfraWorks integration. GCs doing heavy civil work will find Autodesk Build's connection to those design tools more natural than Procore's.

Where Procore has the edge

Financial management

Procore's financial tools — prime contract management, subcontract financial management, change order workflows, and payment applications (G702/703) — are more mature than Autodesk Build's cost module. For commercial GCs where the financial management of multiple subcontracts is a primary operational challenge, Procore's tools are more battle-tested.

RFI and submittal depth

Procore's RFI and submittal workflows have more configuration options, more robust routing rules, and better analytics for tracking cycle times and bottlenecks. On complex commercial projects with active design teams, this depth translates to faster response cycles and better documentation.

Integration ecosystem

Procore's marketplace has 500+ integration partners — more than any competing construction platform. If you use specialized tools for scheduling (Primavera P6), estimating (Sage Estimating), accounting (Sage 300 CRE, Viewpoint Vista, CMiC), or specialty apps, there's a higher chance they connect to Procore than to Autodesk Build.

US market penetration

Procore has the largest commercial construction customer base in the US. This matters practically: owners, architects, and subcontractors on your projects are more likely to already have Procore accounts, reducing the friction of onboarding external collaborators.

The honest recommendation

For most commercial GCs who are evaluating these two platforms fresh, Procore is the safer choice — larger ecosystem, more mature financial tools, and wider industry adoption. The exceptions:

  • Companies already deep in the Autodesk design ecosystem who want native BIM integration
  • Infrastructure and heavy civil contractors where Civil 3D/InfraWorks integration matters
  • Organizations where design coordination (BIM clash detection, model-linked field issues) is a primary use case

Pricing reality

Both platforms require custom pricing conversations. Neither publishes rates. Based on market data, comparable implementations tend to be in similar price ranges — the cost difference depends more on which modules you include than on platform-level pricing. Negotiate hard on both; both have room to move on price for competitive situations.

Frequently asked questions

Can I use Autodesk Build without other Autodesk products?

Yes — Autodesk Build works as a standalone construction management platform without requiring Revit or other design tools. But if you're not in the Autodesk design ecosystem, you lose the primary advantage Autodesk Build has over Procore. For companies that aren't using Autodesk design tools, Procore is usually the stronger choice.

Is Procore losing market share to Autodesk?

Autodesk has been aggressively growing its construction cloud business and taking share in certain segments (particularly BIM-heavy and infrastructure). But as of 2026, Procore maintains the largest US market share in commercial construction management. Both are significant players and both are investing heavily in product development.

What about mid-size companies choosing between them?

For mid-size commercial GCs ($10–$50M annual revenue), both platforms are viable but neither is cheap. Do a proper proof-of-concept with both, involve your project engineers and supers in the evaluation, and weight the decision toward whichever platform your project team will actually adopt and use consistently.

Buildertrend — Not the right fit for heavy commercial work, but if any part of your business is residential, Buildertrend is the platform to evaluate for that segment.
